chunk_summary: Management of dysentery in severe acute malnutrition involves prompt antibiotics, cautious rehydration, and continued feeding, with escalation if no improvement in 48 hours.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of empiric antibiotics in dysentery management for SAM patients.
Learn appropriate rehydration protocols for malnourished children with dysentery.
Recognize escalation criteria and alternative diagnoses in persistent dysentery cases.
key_facts: Empiric antibiotics: Oral ciprofloxacin 15 mg/kg twice daily for 3 days; parenteral ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg/day for 5 days if oral therapy is not tolerated.
Rehydration: Use malnutrition-appropriate solutions like ReSoMal or diluted low-osmolar ORS with added potassium/glucose, reassessing every 30 minutes initially.
Escalation: Reassess adherence, dosing, and consider resistant shigellosis or alternative diagnoses if no improvement within 48 hours.
Feeding: Continue breastfeeding and stabilization feeds (F-75) during dysentery management.
Monitor: Stop or slow fluids if respiratory rate or pulse rise from baseline or edema worsens.
reference_quote: Severe malnutrition increases risk of invasive bacterial disease and complications, and dehydration assessment can be unreliable; therefore management must combine prompt antibiotics with cautious, closely monitored rehydration and continued feeding. (WHO Pocket Book, 2011)
chunk_heading: SEVERE ACUTE MALNUTRITION - (SAM) MANAGEMENT;INFECTION MANAGEMENT AND METABOLIC CORRECTIONS;DYSENTERY MANAGEMENT WITH SECOND-LINE ESCALATION;
